preamble.summary: Targum Onkelos on Exodus shapes the narrative of redemption through consistent theological lenses: the Memra initiates and executes liberation, anti-anthropomorphism governs all theophanies (burning bush, Sinai, the cleft of the rock), and Shekinah language defines God's dwelling among Israel. The Poem of Four Nights at Exodus 12:42 is a unique targum composition linking creation, Abraham, Exodus, and Messiah.
preamble.notable_renderings: The burning bush theophany is rendered as revelation rather than appearance. The Song of the Sea celebrates the Memra. Sinai's thunder is the voice of the Memra. Moses' request to see God's glory is carefully managed — he sees the Shekinah's aftereffect, never God's face. The Poem of Four Nights (12:42) is the most distinctive Onkelos/Palestinian targum addition in the entire Torah.
preamble.theological_themes: Memra as the agent of liberation and covenant; Shekinah as God's tabernacling presence (directly connected to the Tabernacle construction); anti-anthropomorphism at every theophany; the eschatological Poem of Four Nights linking Exodus typology to Messianic expectation.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת־נַאֲקָתָם וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת־בְּרִיתוֹ
renderings.targum_text: ushtamia' qodam Adonai yat tzan'athon udekhir Adonai yat keyameih
renderings.targum_rendering: And it was heard before the LORD their groaning, and the LORD remembered his covenant.
renderings.mt_rendering: And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant.
notes: Onkelos uses the passive 'it was heard before the LORD' rather than God actively hearing, a circumlocution that avoids implying God has ears while preserving the theological reality that Israel's cries reached heaven.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיֵּרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה אֵלָיו בְּלַבַּת־אֵשׁ מִתּוֹךְ הַסְּנֶה
renderings.targum_text: ve'itchazi leih malakha daAdonai beshalhoveita de'esha migo'o de'asna
renderings.targum_rendering: And the angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of the bush.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of the bush.
notes: Onkelos preserves the angel as the visible agent at the burning bush, maintaining the intermediary that the Hebrew text itself provides.

renderings.targum_text: uqra leih Adonai migo'o de'asna
renderings.targum_rendering: And the LORD called to him from the midst of the bush.
renderings.mt_rendering: And God called to him from the midst of the bush.
notes: Onkelos does not insert Memra here — God's direct speech from the bush is preserved, consistent with the pattern of allowing first-person divine address.

renderings.targum_text: arei Memri yehei besiyyakhah
renderings.targum_rendering: For my Memra will be in your help.
renderings.mt_rendering: For I will be with you.
notes: The promise of divine presence to Moses is mediated through the Memra, following the patriarchal pattern (Gen 28:15, 39:2). The Memra is the mode of God's accompaniment with his appointed leaders.

renderings.targum_text: Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh
renderings.targum_rendering: I Am Who I Am.
renderings.mt_rendering: I Am Who I Am.
notes: Onkelos preserves the divine name revelation without interpretation, treating it as too sacred to paraphrase. This is remarkable given Onkelos' general willingness to interpret — the Name itself is beyond targum.

renderings.hebrew_text: אֲנִי יְהוָה וָאֵרָא אֶל־אַבְרָהָם... בְּאֵל שַׁדָּי וּשְׁמִי יְהוָה לֹא נוֹדַעְתִּי לָהֶם
renderings.targum_text: ana Adonai ve'itgeleiti le'Avraham... be'El Shadday ushmei Adonai la hodeiti lehon
renderings.targum_rendering: I am the LORD. And I revealed myself to Abraham... as God Almighty, but by my name the LORD I did not make myself known to them.
renderings.mt_rendering: I am the LORD. And I appeared to Abraham... as God Almighty, but by my name the LORD I was not known to them.
notes: The theophany to the patriarchs is rendered as self-revelation (itgeleiti), and knowledge of the Name is described as God's active disclosure (hodeiti) rather than passive recognition.

renderings.targum_text: ve'itgelei be'ar'a deMitzrayim... ana Adonai
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will reveal myself in the land of Egypt... I am the LORD.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will pass through the land of Egypt... I am the LORD.
notes: God does not 'pass through' Egypt like a traveler. Onkelos replaces physical movement with divine self-revelation, consistent with the theology that God acts without spatial displacement.

renderings.targum_text: ve'echzei yat dema ve'echush aleikhon
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will see the blood and I will have mercy upon you.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will see the blood and I will pass over you.
notes: Onkelos interprets 'pass over' (pasach) as 'have mercy' (echush), shifting from a physical action (skipping over houses) to a theological one (exercising mercy). This deepens the Passover from spatial movement to divine compassion.

renderings.targum_text: vela yitten malakha demachei le'me'al levatekhon
renderings.targum_rendering: And he will not allow the destroying angel to enter your houses.
renderings.mt_rendering: And he will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses.
notes: The 'destroyer' (mashchit) is identified as 'the destroying angel' (malakha demachei), an angelic agent rather than an impersonal force. This clarifies the agent of the plague while distancing God from direct destruction.

renderings.targum_text: leilya denetira hu qodam Adonai... arba'a leilavvan
renderings.targum_rendering: It is a night of watching before the LORD... four nights [are recorded in the memorial book].
renderings.mt_rendering: It was a night of watching for the LORD.
notes: This is the Poem of Four Nights, the most famous unique composition in the targum tradition. Onkelos (and more fully Pseudo-Jonathan and Neofiti) expands this verse into a cosmic four-night schema: (1) the night of creation when God's Memra illuminated the darkness, (2) the night of Abraham's covenant/Aqedah, (3) the night of the Exodus, and (4) the night of the Messiah's coming. This links all of salvation history into a single nocturnal typology.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיהוָה הֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵיהֶם יוֹמָם בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן
renderings.targum_text: vaAdonai madbar qodamehon bimama be'amuda da'anana
renderings.targum_rendering: And the LORD was leading before them by day in a pillar of cloud.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the LORD was going before them by day in a pillar of cloud.
notes: 'Going' (holekh) becomes 'leading' (madbar), shifting from physical locomotion to purposeful guidance. God does not walk but directs.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַיָּד הַגְּדֹלָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה יְהוָה בְּמִצְרַיִם
renderings.targum_text: vachaza Yisrael yat gevurta rabbeta da'avad Adonai beMitzrayim
renderings.targum_rendering: And Israel saw the great power which the LORD had wrought in Egypt.
renderings.mt_rendering: And Israel saw the great hand which the LORD had wielded against Egypt.
notes: 'The great hand' becomes 'the great power' (gevurta rabbeta), removing the anthropomorphic image of God's hand and replacing it with an abstract attribute.

renderings.hebrew_text: אָז יָשִׁיר־מֹשֶׁה וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַשִּׁירָה הַזֹּאת לַיהוָה
renderings.targum_text: bedain shabbach Mosheh uvenei Yisrael yat tushbechta hada qodam Adonai
renderings.targum_rendering: Then Moses and the children of Israel sang this song of praise before the LORD.
renderings.mt_rendering: Then Moses and the children of Israel sang this song to the LORD.
notes: The Song of the Sea is described as praise (tushbechta) offered 'before the LORD' (qodam Adonai), aligning the Exodus celebration with synagogue worship patterns.

renderings.targum_text: tushbechti vetiqfi Adonai vahava li lefurqan
renderings.targum_rendering: My praise and my strength is the LORD, and he has become for me redemption.
renderings.mt_rendering: The LORD is my strength and my song, and he has become my salvation.
notes: Salvation (yeshu'ah) is rendered as redemption (furqan), the same Aramaic term used in messianic contexts, linking the Exodus redemption typologically to eschatological deliverance.

renderings.targum_text: Adonai gevur qaravayya Adonai shemeih
renderings.targum_rendering: The LORD is a warrior in battle; the LORD is his name.
renderings.mt_rendering: The LORD is a man of war; the LORD is his name.
notes: 'Man of war' (ish milchamah) becomes 'warrior in battle' (gevur qaravayya), removing the word 'man' (ish) from any description of God while preserving the martial imagery.

renderings.targum_text: yeminakh Adonai meshabbachta becheilah
renderings.targum_rendering: Your right hand, O LORD, is glorious in power.
renderings.mt_rendering: Your right hand, O LORD, is glorious in power.
notes: Onkelos preserves 'right hand' in the poetic Song of the Sea, treating it as established liturgical language. The targum tradition is more tolerant of anthropomorphism in poetry than in narrative.

renderings.hebrew_text: כִּי־יָד עַל־כֵּס יָהּ מִלְחָמָה לַיהוָה בַּעֲמָלֵק
renderings.targum_text: arei bishevuah min qodam kurseih deyeqara daAdonai qerav laAdonai ba'Amaleq
renderings.targum_rendering: For by an oath from before the throne of the glory of the LORD, the LORD will wage war against Amalek.
renderings.mt_rendering: For a hand upon the throne of the LORD — war for the LORD against Amalek.
notes: The cryptic 'hand upon the throne of Yah' is interpreted as a solemn oath from before God's glorious throne. Onkelos transforms a physical gesture into a juridical act, and introduces the 'glory' (yeqar) as a buffer for direct reference to God's throne.

renderings.hebrew_text: וּמֹשֶׁה עָלָה אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה מִן־הָהָר
renderings.targum_text: uMosheh seliq leqodam Adonai uqra leih Adonai min tura
renderings.targum_rendering: And Moses went up before the LORD, and the LORD called to him from the mountain.
renderings.mt_rendering: And Moses went up to God, and the LORD called to him from the mountain.
notes: Moses goes up 'before the LORD' (leqodam Adonai) rather than 'to God,' using the reverential circumlocution that avoids implying God is spatially located on the mountain.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיּוֹצֵא מֹשֶׁה אֶת־הָעָם לִקְרַאת הָאֱלֹהִים מִן־הַמַּחֲנֶה
renderings.targum_text: ve'appeiq Mosheh yat amma le'araa shekhinteih daAdonai min mashrita
renderings.targum_rendering: And Moses brought out the people to meet the Shekinah of the LORD from the camp.
renderings.mt_rendering: And Moses brought the people out to meet God from the camp.
notes: Israel does not meet God directly but the Shekinah of the LORD. This is one of the most significant Shekinah passages in Onkelos — the Sinai theophany is an encounter with God's indwelling presence, not God's essence.

renderings.hebrew_text: מִפְּנֵי אֲשֶׁר יָרַד עָלָיו יְהוָה בָּאֵשׁ
renderings.targum_text: min qodam de'itgeli aloi Adonai be'esha
renderings.targum_rendering: Because the LORD revealed himself upon it in fire.
renderings.mt_rendering: Because the LORD descended upon it in fire.
notes: God does not 'descend' upon Sinai. The descent is replaced with revelation (itgeli), preserving divine transcendence even at the most dramatic theophany in the Torah.

renderings.targum_text: ve'itgeli Adonai al tura deSinai
renderings.targum_rendering: And the LORD revealed himself upon Mount Sinai.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the LORD came down upon Mount Sinai.
notes: Repeated rendering: every instance of divine descent at Sinai is transformed into divine revelation. Onkelos is systematic and consistent in this substitution.

renderings.targum_text: umalil Adonai yat kol pitgamayya ha'ilein
renderings.targum_rendering: And the LORD spoke all these words.
renderings.mt_rendering: And God spoke all these words.
notes: At the giving of the Ten Commandments, Onkelos uses 'the LORD' rather than Elohim, emphasizing that the covenant God of Israel (YHWH) — not a generic deity — delivers the commandments.

renderings.targum_text: vekhol amma chazin yat qalayya
renderings.targum_rendering: And all the people were seeing the thunderings.
renderings.mt_rendering: And all the people were seeing the thunderings.
notes: Onkelos preserves the synesthetic 'seeing the thunderings,' which rabbinic tradition interprets as the people seeing the voice of God — each commandment emerging as visible fire.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיַּעַמְדוּ מֵרָחֹק... וְאַל־יְדַבֵּר עִמָּנוּ אֱלֹהִים פֶּן־נָמוּת
renderings.targum_text: veqamu merachiq... vela yitmalal immana min qodam Adonai dilma nemut
renderings.targum_rendering: And they stood afar off... and let not [a word] be spoken with us from before the LORD, lest we die.
renderings.mt_rendering: And they stood afar off... and let not God speak with us, lest we die.
notes: The people's fear of God speaking directly is rendered as fear of speech 'from before the LORD,' adding the reverential circumlocution even in the people's own words.

renderings.targum_text: vechazo yat yeqara de'Elaha deYisrael
renderings.targum_rendering: And they saw the glory of the God of Israel.
renderings.mt_rendering: And they saw the God of Israel.
notes: This is one of the most critical anti-anthropomorphic moves in all of Onkelos. The elders cannot have 'seen God' — they saw 'the glory (yeqar) of the God of Israel.' No human sees God directly; they see the kavod/yeqar.

renderings.targum_text: vechazo yat yeqara daAdonai vechadiu be'qorbanehon de'itqabilu keilu akhalu veshatiu
renderings.targum_rendering: And they saw the glory of the LORD, and they rejoiced in their offerings which were accepted as though they ate and drank.
renderings.mt_rendering: And they beheld God, and they ate and drank.
notes: Onkelos cannot allow the casual statement that the elders saw God and then had a meal. The seeing is redirected to God's glory, and the eating/drinking is reinterpreted as joy over accepted sacrifices — transforming a covenant meal into a liturgical celebration.

renderings.targum_text: veya'avdun qodomi maqdasha ve'ashrei Shekhineti beineihon
renderings.targum_rendering: And let them make before me a sanctuary, and I will cause my Shekinah to dwell among them.
renderings.mt_rendering: And let them make me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them.
notes: The foundational Tabernacle verse: God does not dwell among Israel directly but causes his Shekinah to dwell among them. This establishes the Shekinah as the mode of divine indwelling in sacred space — the theological heart of the Tabernacle.

renderings.hebrew_text: וְשָׁכַנְתִּי בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְהָיִיתִי לָהֶם לֵאלֹהִים
renderings.targum_text: ve'ashrei Shekhineti bego benei Yisrael ve'ehei lehon l'Elah
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will cause my Shekinah to dwell among the children of Israel, and I will be their God.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will dwell among the children of Israel and will be their God.
notes: Repeated Shekinah formula: God's indwelling is consistently expressed through the Shekinah. The covenant formula 'I will be their God' is preserved without mediation — the relationship is direct even if the mode of presence is the Shekinah.

renderings.hebrew_text: וְיָדְעוּ כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִי אֹתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לְשָׁכְנִי בְתוֹכָם
renderings.targum_text: viyeid'un arei ana Adonai Elahehon de'appiqit yathon me'ar'a deMitzrayim le'ashraah Shekhineti beineihon
renderings.targum_rendering: And they shall know that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out from the land of Egypt, to cause my Shekinah to dwell among them.
renderings.mt_rendering: And they shall know that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out of the land of Egypt, that I might dwell among them.
notes: The purpose of the Exodus is Shekinah-dwelling. Onkelos ties liberation directly to divine presence: God freed Israel so that his Shekinah could tabernacle in their midst.

renderings.targum_text: arei ata hi bein Memri uveineikhon
renderings.targum_rendering: For it is a sign between my Memra and you.
renderings.mt_rendering: For it is a sign between me and you.
notes: The Sabbath sign, like the rainbow sign (Gen 9:12), is placed between the Memra and Israel rather than between God directly and Israel. Covenant signs are mediated through the Word.

renderings.targum_text: beyoma shevi'a'ah shevat venach
renderings.targum_rendering: On the seventh day he ceased and rested.
renderings.mt_rendering: On the seventh day he ceased and was refreshed.
notes: The Hebrew vayinnafash ('was refreshed,' from nefesh/soul) is softened to 'rested' (nach), removing any implication that God needed refreshment or has a soul that required restoration.

renderings.targum_text: ukh'an sh'voq min qodamai viyitqaf rug'zi behon
renderings.targum_rendering: And now, desist from before me, and let my anger be strong against them.
renderings.mt_rendering: Now therefore let me alone, that my anger may burn against them.
notes: 'Let me alone' becomes 'desist from before me,' and 'anger burning' becomes 'anger being strong' (yitqaf) — Onkelos subtly adjusts the metaphors to reduce the anthropomorphic intensity while preserving the drama of the Golden Calf crisis.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה עַל־הָרָעָה אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר לַעֲשׂוֹת לְעַמּוֹ
renderings.targum_text: vetav qodam Adonai al bishta di malil leme'bad le'ammeih
renderings.targum_rendering: And it was good before the LORD concerning the evil which he had spoken to do to his people.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the LORD relented concerning the evil which he had spoken of doing to his people.
notes: God does not 'relent' or 'change his mind' in Onkelos. The anthropopathic verb nacham is replaced with 'it was good before the LORD,' a reverential formula that preserves divine immutability.

renderings.targum_text: Shekhineti tehakh ve'anikha lakh
renderings.targum_rendering: My Shekinah shall go, and I will give you rest.
renderings.mt_rendering: My presence shall go, and I will give you rest.
notes: 'My face' (panai, literally 'my faces') is rendered 'my Shekinah.' God's face — the most intimate anthropomorphism — becomes the Shekinah, the divine presence that accompanies Israel without compromising God's transcendence.

renderings.hebrew_text: לֹא תוּכַל לִרְאֹת אֶת־פָּנָי כִּי לֹא־יִרְאַנִי הָאָדָם וָחָי
renderings.targum_text: la tikdar lemechzei yat appai arei la yechzinnani enasha veyeqayyam
renderings.targum_rendering: You are not able to see my face, for no man shall see me and live.
renderings.mt_rendering: You cannot see my face, for no man shall see me and live.
notes: Remarkably, Onkelos preserves 'my face' here rather than substituting Shekinah or glory. The point is prohibition: no one can see God's face. The literal rendering reinforces the impossibility of direct vision of God, which is the very principle that drives Onkelos' anti-anthropomorphism everywhere else.

renderings.targum_text: vetechzei yat di vatrai ve'appai la yitchazun
renderings.targum_rendering: And you shall see what is behind me, but my face shall not be seen.
renderings.mt_rendering: And you shall see my back, but my face shall not be seen.
notes: 'My back' becomes 'what is behind me' (di vatrai), subtly shifting from a body part to a spatial/temporal concept — what follows after God's passing, not God's physical backside.

renderings.targum_text: ve'itgeli Adonai be'anana
renderings.targum_rendering: And the LORD revealed himself in the cloud.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the LORD descended in the cloud.
notes: God does not descend into the cloud but reveals himself in it. The cloud remains the medium of theophany, but divine movement is replaced with self-disclosure.

renderings.targum_text: Adonai Adonai Elaha rachama vechanina
renderings.targum_rendering: The LORD, the LORD, God compassionate and gracious.
renderings.mt_rendering: The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious.
notes: The Thirteen Attributes of Mercy are rendered with minimal change. Onkelos treats this divine self-description as authoritative and does not adjust the attributive language — these qualities describe God's character, not his body.

renderings.targum_text: tehakh ke'an Shekhinteih daAdonai begavvana
renderings.targum_rendering: Let the Shekinah of the LORD go now in our midst.
renderings.mt_rendering: Let the Lord go in our midst.
notes: Moses' plea for God's presence is a plea for the Shekinah. God does not 'go' in Israel's midst — the Shekinah does. This is one of the clearest Shekinah passages in Exodus.

renderings.hebrew_text: וַיְכַס הֶעָנָן אֶת־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וּכְבוֹד יְהוָה מָלֵא אֶת־הַמִּשְׁכָּן
renderings.targum_text: vekhassa anana yat mashkan ziman viyeqara daAdonai malei yat mishkena
renderings.targum_rendering: And the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the Tabernacle.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the Tabernacle.
notes: At the climax of Exodus, Onkelos renders 'glory' (kavod/yeqar) literally. The glory filling the Tabernacle is the culmination of the Shekinah theology developed throughout the book — God's presence has come to dwell among his people.

renderings.hebrew_text: וְלֹא־יָכֹל מֹשֶׁה לָבוֹא אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד כִּי־שָׁכַן עָלָיו הֶעָנָן
renderings.targum_text: vela yakhil Mosheh le'me'al lemashkan zimna arei sherat aloi anana
renderings.targum_rendering: And Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting, because the cloud had settled upon it.
renderings.mt_rendering: And Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting, because the cloud had settled upon it.
notes: The verb sherat ('settled/rested') replaces shakhan ('dwelt'), using a term that emphasizes the cloud's tangible presence. The Shekinah is so powerfully manifest that even Moses cannot enter.
